sources.label: Supreme Court of India — Official Website

bias_complaint_to.mechanism: In-House Procedure (SC Resolution 1999). Complaint to CJI. CJI constitutes 3-judge committee. Report is confidential. Complainant has no locus in inquiry proceedings.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Impeachment — Article 124(4). Motion by 100+ LS MPs or 50+ RS MPs. Never successfully completed in Indian history.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: Lokpal ruled in Jan 2025 that it cannot hear complaints against SC judges because the SC is established by Article 124 (Constitution), not an Act of Parliament. SC stayed Lokpal's attempt to assert jurisdiction over HC judges (suo moto, Feb 2025). Status: active litigation.
complaint_mechanism.source: K. Veeraswami v Union of India (1991) 3 SCC 655; SC In-House Procedure 1999; SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto)

digital_infrastructure.integration_notes: NJDG-SC portal launched in 2023 as part of eCourts Phase III. SC data was the last to be integrated into national NJDG.

judge_strength.notes: Sanctioned/working/vacancy from row A; no Pmt/Addl split shown for SC

entities.name: High Court of Delhi
entities.constitutional_basis: Constitution of India, Chapter VI — High Courts in the States
entities.data_quality_notes: Pending NJDG-backed pendency and judge_strength fields per bench. NJDG snapshot case_volume merged complaint_mechanism populated from SC In-House Procedure 1999 and SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto). criminal_prosecution.requires_sanction_from set to governor_state per Veeraswami (1991) for HC judges. lokpal_jurisdiction No per operative Feb 2025 stay; revisit if SC decides finally. audit.conduct_oversight_body set to in_house_procedure_sc (same procedure governs HC judges).

bias_complaint_to.mechanism: In-House Procedure (SC Resolution 1999, extended to HC judges). Complaint lodged with the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court; if the allegation is against that Chief Justice, complaint filed directly with the CJI. CJI may constitute a 3-judge in-house committee to inquire. Report is confidential. Complainant has no locus in inquiry proceedings.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Address by Parliament — Article 217(1)(b) read with Article 124(4). Motion by 100+ LS MPs or 50+ RS MPs. No HC judge has been successfully removed in Indian history.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: SC stayed Lokpal's attempt to assert jurisdiction over HC judges (suo moto, Feb 2025). Lokpal had separately ruled in Jan 2025 that it cannot hear complaints against SC judges (Art 124 basis); the Feb 2025 stay extends the same bar to HC judges. Status: active litigation, but operative bar currently in place.
complaint_mechanism.source: K. Veeraswami v Union of India (1991) 3 SCC 655; SC In-House Procedure 1999; SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto re HC judges)

judge_strength.notes: Pmt 45 Addl 15 sanctioned; Pmt 44 Addl 0 working; Pmt 1 Addl 15 vacancies

entities.name: Board for Advance Rulings (Income Tax)
entities.statutory_basis: Income Tax Act 1961, Chapter XIX-B (ss.245N–245V) as substituted by Finance Act 2021 s.98 — reconstituted as 'Board for Advance Rulings' from 'Authority for Advance Rulings'. Two Boards constituted with retired ITAT Presidents/Vice Presidents. Original AAR (single-member, retired SC Judge as Chairman) abolished by Finance Act 2021 w.e.f. 01.04.2021.
entities.data_quality_notes: Independence concern: Finance Act 2021 reconstitution replaced retired SC Judge Chairman with senior IRS officers — structural shift from judicial to departmental composition. Contested by taxpayer bar (pending writ petitions in several HCs challenging constitutionality of non-judicial composition for advance rulings).   Two Boards at Delhi and Mumbai constituted by CBDT Notification No. 96/2021 (S.O. 3561(E)) dated 1 September 2021 — corrected from prior erroneous reference to 92/2021 (drt_b4). Rulings binding on applicant and CBDT (s.245S) but subject to writ jurisdiction of HC under Art.226/227. No appeal to ITAT or HC as of right. created_year reflects original AAR (Finance Act 1993 Chapter XIX-B); reconstitution year 2021. v1.2 judge_strength stub attached

unverified_fields.note: Direct incometaxindia.gov.in PDF for Notification 96/2021 returns 404; S.O. 3561(E) text confirmed via taxguru.in transcript.

sources.label: Finance Act 2021 — s.98 substituting Chapter XIX-B of IT Act 1961
Income Tax Act 1961 — Chapter XIX-B (ss.245N–245V as amended)
CBDT Notification No. 96/2021 (S.O. 3561(E)) dated 1 September 2021 — constitutes Board for Advance Rulings at Delhi and Mumbai; AAR ceases operation from 1 September 2021
Taxmann summary — Notification No. 96/2021 and 97/2021 dated 1 September 2021

appointment.tenure: Board Chairman: retired officer of Indian Revenue Service (IRS) at Commissioner level or above. Members: IRS officers. Tenure and terms as prescribed under s.245-OB rules. Contrast pre-2021 AAR where Chairman was retired SC Judge.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: As per IT Act s.245-OB and rules; members are IRS officers subject to service rules and departmental proceedings. No independent removal committee.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: Board members are public servants (IRS officers) and subject to Lokpal jurisdiction unlike judicial members of pre-2021 AAR.
complaint_mechanism.source: IT Act 1961 s.245-OB; Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act 2013 s.14

unverified_fields.note: Direct incometaxindia.gov.in PDF for Notification 96/2021 returns 404; S.O. 3561(E) text confirmed via taxguru.in transcript.

entities.name: Airports Economic Regulatory Authority of India

jurisdiction_types: Airport tariff and user development fee determination

entities.name_hindi: भारतीय हवाई अड्डा आर्थिक विनियामक प्राधिकरण
entities.statutory_basis: Airports Economic Regulatory Authority of India Act 2008 (§ 3 — establishment; § 17 — appellate tribunal reference to TDSAT).
entities.data_quality_notes: Appellate path: AERA tariff order → TDSAT (Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal, which also has jurisdiction over AERA appeals per AERA Act § 17) → Supreme Court. TDSAT acting as AERA Appellate Tribunal is a shared-body arrangement confirmed by Ministry of Civil Aviation notifications. AERA's quasi-judicial function is tariff determination, not case adjudication; no NJDG-integrated pendency data. case_volume deferred to Phase-2.

sources.label: Airports Economic Regulatory Authority of India Act 2008

appointment.tenure: Five years or age 65, whichever earlier (Chairperson)

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Removal by Central Government on grounds specified in AERA Act 2008 § 8.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: AERA Chairperson is a public servant; Lokpal jurisdiction applies under Lokpal & Lokayuktas Act 2013 § 14.

jurisdiction_types: Airport tariff and user development fee determination

entities.name: Armed Forces Tribunal
entities.statutory_basis: Armed Forces Tribunal Act 2007 (Act 55 of 2007); commenced 8 August 2009 via Gazette notification. Jurisdiction covers service matters of Army, Navy, Air Force personnel and appeals from court-martial convictions/sentences under Army Act 1950, Navy Act 1957, Air Force Act 1950.
entities.data_quality_notes: Entity created from statutory sources. Bench lattice: 11 locations (Principal Bench New Delhi + 10 regional benches) but 15 sitting courts total — Delhi, Chandigarh, and Lucknow each have 3 courts; other regional benches have 1 court each (PIB 2009; aftdelhi.nic.in). README shorthand AFT×11 = 11 location entities, not 15 courts. Note: AFT appeals go directly to the Supreme Court (AFT Act 2007, s.30) — NOT to High Courts. Member vacancy is bench-specific; no verified aggregate vacancy percentage — do not cite a single %. NJDG snapshot case_volume merged v1.2 judge_strength stub attached

sources.label: AFT official website — bench structure (11 locations, 15 courts)
PIB — AFT inaugurated; 15 courts across Delhi, Chandigarh, Lucknow and regional benches

appointment.tenure: Chairperson (retd. SC/HC judge): 4 years or age 70; Members: 4 years or age 65; no reappointment

complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: AFT members are retired judges; Lokpal Act 2013 excludes judicial officers.
complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Removal by President on grounds of misbehaviour after inquiry by a judge of the Supreme Court (AFT Act 2007, s.11)

judge_strength.notes: Multiple Regional Benches (Principal Bench Delhi + 8 regional benches). Total sanctioned and working member strength not verified at authoring time.

gaps.gap_description: No High Court appeal path — appeals lie directly to Supreme Court under AFT Act 2007 s.30 (SLP route).
Ministry of Defence nominates members, funds the tribunal, and is the respondent in service-matter litigation — appointer-litigant loop.
gaps.gap_source: AFT Act 2007; MoD as primary funder and nominating authority

gaps.gap_description: Bench-by-bench vacancies (Rajya Sabha, Mar 2026): Jabalpur, Guwahati, Srinagar each 2/2 vacant; Chandigarh, Lucknow, Kochi, Chennai, Kolkata each 1 vacancy; Principal Bench Delhi fully staffed; 11 of 34 member posts vacant nationally. No single aggregate % — use bench table.
gaps.gap_source: MoS Defence Sanjay Seth, Rajya Sabha reply, 30 Mar 2026

relationships.notes: Awards from IIAC (formerly NDIAC) arbitration proceedings challenged under Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 s.34. IIAC seat is New Delhi; Delhi High Court is the competent court under A&C Act s.2(1)(e)(i). Appeals from s.34 orders lie to HC Division Bench under s.37.

sources.label: Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 — India Code

relationships.notes: Awards from DIAC arbitration proceedings challenged under A&C Act 1996 s.34 in Delhi High Court. DIAC is administered by Delhi HC itself — s.34 challenge venue is Delhi HC by both seat and administration.

sources.label: Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 — India Code

relationships.notes: The Collegium system is entirely judge-made — not written in the Constitution. Article 124(2) says President appoints "in consultation with" CJI and other judges. SC in Second Judges Case (1993) held this consultation is binding; CJI's recommendation is determinative. This reversed SP Gupta (1981) which had held executive supremacy. The 99th Amendment NJAC (2014) attempted to change this but was struck down in Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record v Union of India (2015).

sources.label: Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record v Union of India (1993) 4 SCC 441

relationships.notes: Formal instrument of appointment issued by President. In practice, President acts on Collegium's recommendation. Post-1993, President cannot appoint a judge the Collegium has not recommended.

NJAC was constituted by 99th Constitutional Amendment. Would have replaced the Collegium with a 6-member commission including CJI, 2 senior SC judges, Law Minister, and 2 eminent persons. Struck down by SC 5-judge bench in Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record v Union of India (2015) as violating the basic structure of judicial independence. This relationship is HISTORICAL — rendered as faded/ghosted in the UI when time slider is set after 2015.

sources.label: Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record v Union of India (2015) 5 SCC 1

relationships.notes: CJI or a Supreme Court judge nominated by the CJI is a statutory member of the CBI Director selection committee.

General Court Martial (GCM), District Court Martial (DCM) and Summary General Court Martial (SGCM) orders are appealable to AFT. Summary Court Martial (SCM) orders are NOT appealable to AFT — only revision/habeas corpus routes apply (Union of India v. SPS Vains, 2008 — citation unverified).
TDSAT acts as the appellate tribunal for AERA orders.
State Election Commissioner may be removed only on grounds of misbehaviour, after inquiry by the Supreme Court — protection analogous to a High Court judge. Governor acts on SC inquiry outcome.
CJI chairs NJA Governing Council; academy works under directions of the Supreme Court despite GoI funding via DoJ.
e-Committee is constituted by the Chief Justice of India for court ICT policy.
Legacy e-Committee entity id — same SC policy oversight pattern.
SC appellate jurisdiction over court martial (pre-AFT Act 2007 framework).
SC In-House Procedure governs judicial conduct complaints for SC and HC judges.

AFT Act 2007, s.30 confines further appeal to the Supreme Court. High Courts have no appellate or writ jurisdiction over AFT orders (this was upheld by SC in Union of India v. Major General Shri Kant Sharma). This is a deliberate statutory design to centralise appeals.
